elements.sent-2411-001: We propose a gradient-based simulated parameter estimation framework, leveraging a multi-time scale algorithm that tackles the issue of ratio bias in both maximum likelihood estimation and posterior density estimation problems.
elements.sent-2411-002: Additionally, we introduce a nested simulation optimization structure, providing theoretical analyses including strong convergence, asymptotic normality, convergence rate, and budget allocation strategies for the proposed algorithm.
elements.sent-2411-003: The framework is further extended to neural network training, offering a novel perspective on stochastic approximation in machine learning.
elements.sent-2411-004: Numerical experiments show that our algorithm can improve the estimation accuracy and save computational costs.
elements.sent-2411-005: Parameter estimation plays a crucial role in fields such as financial risk assessment and medical diagnosis, where it involves calibrating model parameters based on observed data.
elements.sent-2411-006: The frequentist approach treats parameters as unknown values, while the Bayesian approach infers their posterior distribution.
elements.sent-2411-007: Key inference methods include maximum likelihood estimation (MLE), which offers consistency and asymptotic efficiency \citep{shao2003mathematical , and posterior density estimation (PDE), which integrates observed data with prior knowledge for accurate inference.}
elements.sent-2411-008: Both have been widely used in statistics and machine learning.
elements.sent-2411-009: The solution to MLE relies on the analytical form of the logarithmic likelihood function.
elements.sent-2411-010: By substituting the observed data and solving for its maximum value, the MLE can be obtained.
elements.sent-2411-011: For PDE, the classical approach is variational inference \citep{blei2017variational , which similarly requires an analytical form of the logarithmic likelihood.}
elements.sent-2411-012: This method assumes a family of posterior distributions and minimizes the Kullback-Leibler divergence (KL divergence) to derive the optimal posterior parameters.
elements.sent-2411-013: This paper focuses on stochastic models or simulators characterized by system dynamics rather than explicit likelihood functions.
elements.sent-2411-014: Examples include Lindley's recursion in queuing models, where the likelihood function of the output data lacks an analytical form, posing significant challenges for parameter calibration.
elements.sent-2411-015: This problem in the MLE case was first proposed and solved by the gradient-based simulated maximum likelihood estimation (GSMLE) method in \cite{Peng2020 .}
elements.sent-2411-016: The Robbins-Monro algorithm, a classic stochastic approximation (SA) algorithm \citep{Kushner1997StochasticAA , is applied to optimize unknown parameters for MLE.}
elements.sent-2411-017: Specifically, let $Y$ represent the observed data and $\theta \in \mathbb{R ^d$ be the parameter of interest, and $p$ denote the unknown density.}
elements.sent-2411-018: The gradient of the logarithm likelihood function $\sum_{t=1 ^{T}\log p(Y_t;\theta)$ with respect to $\theta$ takes the form of a ratio: \begin{equation}\label{eq1} \nabla_{\theta} \sum_{t=1}^{T}\log p(Y_t;\theta) = \sum_{t=1}^{T}\frac{\nabla_{\theta}p(Y_t;\theta)}{p(Y_t;\theta)}.\end{equation}.}
elements.sent-2411-019: In the absence of an analytical form for the likelihood function, the generalized likelihood ratio (GLR) method is employed to get the unbiased estimators for the density and its gradients \citep{peng2018new .}
elements.sent-2411-020: The GLR estimator offers unbiased estimators for “distribution sensitivities” in \cite{Lei2018ApplicationsOG and it achieves a square-root convergence rate \citep{glynn2021computing .}}
elements.sent-2411-021: However, the gradient estimator of the logarithm likelihood function provided in \cite{Peng2020 is not unbiased.}
elements.sent-2411-022: Although the GLR estimator is unbiased, which means we can obtain the unbiased estimators $G_1(Y_t,\theta)$ and $G_2(Y_t,\theta)$ for $\nabla_{\theta p(Y_t;\theta)$ and $p(Y_t;\theta)$ through the GLR method and Monte Carlo simulation, the ratio of these two unbiased estimators may not necessarily be unbiased.}
elements.sent-2411-023: Consequently, when this ratio estimator is used in the Robbins-Monro algorithm, the update becomes: \begin{equation}\label{single} \theta_{k+1} = \theta_k + \beta_k\sum\limits_{t=1}^T\frac{G_1(Y_t,\theta_k)}{G_2(Y_t,\theta_k)},\end{equation} where the gradient term is biased, introducing a certain bias into the iterative results ($\beta_k$ is the step-size, satisfying specific step-size conditions).
elements.sent-2411-024: Also, the estimator on the denominator causes numerical instability, leading to inaccuracies in the MLE.
elements.sent-2411-025: On the other hand, in the context of PDE, the calculation of the log-likelihood function is equally critical.
elements.sent-2411-026: When an analytical form of the likelihood function is unavailable, an estimator must be sought.
elements.sent-2411-027: In this simulation-based inference, also known as likelihood-free inference scenario, traditional methods include approximate Bayesian computation (ABC) and synthetic likelihood methods \citep{tavare1997inferring .}
elements.sent-2411-028: Techniques like variational Bayes synthetic likelihood \citep{ong2018variational and multilevel Monte Carlo variational Bayes \citep{he2022unbiased have been applied to likelihood-free models, such as the g-and-k distribution and the $\alpha$-stable model \citep{peters2012likelihood , but not to stochastic models.}}}
elements.sent-2411-029: Additionally, these methods often require carefully designed summary statistics and distance functions.
elements.sent-2411-030: Meanwhile, in the realm of computer science, numerous approaches leverage neural networks to estimate likelihoods or posteriors that are otherwise infeasible to solve \citep{glöckler2022variationalmethodssimulationbasedinference, pmlr-v97-greenberg19a, papamakarios2019sequential, NIPS2017_6f1d0705 .}
elements.sent-2411-031: However, the likelihood functions inferred through neural networks tend to be biased.
elements.sent-2411-032: The incorporation of neural networks and the presence of such bias render these algorithms challenging from a theoretical standpoint.
elements.sent-2411-033: To simplify this and make theoretical analysis feasible, we put this problem under the umbrella of the SA perspective, using unbiased GLR gradient estimators for the likelihood function as the MLE case.
elements.sent-2411-034: Since the gradient estimator of the posterior density also involves Equation (\ref{eq1 ), how to reduce ratio bias in these stochastic models remains to be explored.}
elements.sent-2411-035: To address the issue of ratio bias that arises in both MLE and PDE problems, we propose a gradient-based simulated parameter estimation (GSPE) algorithm framework based on a multi-time scale (MTS) SA algorithm \citep{Kushner1997StochasticAA,borkar2009stochastic .}
elements.sent-2411-036: The fundamental concept involves treating both the parameters and the gradient of the logarithm likelihood function jointly as components of a stochastic root-finding problem aimed at solving a system of nonlinear equations.
elements.sent-2411-037: Subsequently, the approach tries to approximate the solution by devising two separate but coupled iterations, wherein one component is updated at a faster pace compared to the other.
elements.sent-2411-038: Specifically, we find a recursive estimator that substitutes the ratio form of a gradient estimator.
elements.sent-2411-039: This method enables incremental adjustments of gradient estimators by averaging all accessible simulation data, thereby eliminating the ratio bias throughout the iterative process.
elements.sent-2411-040: Similar methods have been put into the quantile optimization, black-box CoVaR estimation, and dynamic pricing and replenishment problems \citep{hu2022stochastic,hu2024quantile,jiang2023quantile,Cao2023BlackboxCA,zheng2024dual .}
elements.sent-2411-041: However, our work involves a more complex structure, where convergence results are established for two layers, with uniform convergence playing a pivotal role in facilitating this process.
elements.sent-2411-042: The problem in the PDE case is formulated as a nested simulation optimization through the variational inference method.
elements.sent-2411-043: Minimizing KL divergence can be transferred to maximizing the evidence lower bound (ELBO), which is expressed as an expectation with respect to the unknown variational distribution.
elements.sent-2411-044: Consequently, the objective to be optimized is an expectation, and the sample average approximation (SAA) method is used to obtain an unbiased gradient estimator of the ELBO, forming the outer layer simulation.
elements.sent-2411-045: Meanwhile, the intractable likelihood within this expectation is estimated through the inner layer simulation using unbiased GLR estimators.
elements.sent-2411-046: A nested MTS algorithm is designed to address ratio bias, thereby solving the nested simulation optimization problem.
elements.sent-2411-047: Nested simulation is a classic problem that has been explored in simulation literature, with a focus on the consistency and asymptotic normality of estimators \citep{gordy2010nested, Hong2017KernelSF, feng2024efficient .}
elements.sent-2411-048: These theoretical properties guide efficient sampling and budget allocation.
elements.sent-2411-049: However, most studies focus mainly on estimator properties without considering gradients or optimization.
elements.sent-2411-050: Few works tackle the integration of nested simulation and optimization \citep{cakmak2021solving, ghadimi2020single .}
elements.sent-2411-051: To the best of our knowledge, this work is the first to study the nested simulation optimization problem where optimization and nested estimation are performed simultaneously, leveraging a nested MTS algorithm.
elements.sent-2411-052: Furthermore, we introduce the idea of MTS into neural network training to exhibit the compatibility and scalability of our GSPE framework.
elements.sent-2411-053: For overly complex simulators where GLR regularity conditions are not satisfied, we use a neural network as an alternative to estimate the intractable likelihood.
elements.sent-2411-054: Also, when the posterior is complicated and the simple variational distribution family has the limited representative ability, another neural network can serve as the variational distribution.
elements.sent-2411-055: We design an MTS algorithm that adjusts the update frequency of the two neural networks to ensure convergence and improve training outcomes.
elements.sent-2411-056: Our approach provides theoretical support for such estimation and optimization algorithms that need to be updated at different frequencies.
elements.sent-2411-057: More generally, this offers a new SA perspective on neural network training at various scales.
elements.sent-2411-058: Lastly, we address model misspecification; when data deviates from the assumed model, our GSPE framework ensures accuracy in terms of output performance measures.
elements.sent-2411-059: We summarize our main contributions as follows:.
elements.sent-2411-060: We propose a new GSPE framework that can asymptotically eliminate ratio bias for parameter estimation without requiring an analytical likelihood function.
elements.sent-2411-061: The MTS algorithm is applied in the MLE problem, enhancing estimation accuracy and reducing computational cost.
elements.sent-2411-062: The GSPE framework incorporates a nested MTS algorithm to address the PDE problem in conjunction with variational inference, combining SA perspectives into machine learning problems.
elements.sent-2411-063: A nested simulation optimization structure is introduced and analyzed theoretically.
elements.sent-2411-064: We establish strong convergence, $\mathbb{L ^1$ convergence, and the CLT for the nested MTS algorithm framework.}
elements.sent-2411-065: The optimal $\mathbb{L ^1$ convergence rate is derived to guide budget allocation in the algorithm.}
elements.sent-2411-066: The rest of the paper is organized as follows.
elements.sent-2411-067: Section \ref{section2 provides the necessary background and introduces the GSPE algorithm framework for both MLE and PDE cases.}
elements.sent-2411-068: In Section \ref{sec3 , we conduct an in-depth analysis of the algorithm, establishing consistency results, convergence rate, and budget allocation strategies.}
elements.sent-2411-069: Section \ref{sec4 extends the GSPE framework to neural network training.}
elements.sent-2411-070: Section \ref{sec5 presents numerical results, and Section \ref{sec6 concludes the paper.}}
elements.sent-2411-071: This section introduces the basic problem setting for the GSPE framework.
elements.sent-2411-072: To eliminate ratio bias in the MLE problem, we propose the MTS algorithm in Section \ref{section2.1 .}
elements.sent-2411-073: Additionally, a nested MTS approach is introduced for the PDE problem in Section \ref{section2.2 .}
elements.sent-2411-074: Considering a stochastic model, let $X$ be a random variable with density function $f(x,\theta)$ where $\theta \in \mathbb{R ^d$ is the parameter with feasible domain $\Theta\subset\mathbb{R ^d$.}}
elements.sent-2411-075: Another random variable $Y$ is defined by the relationship $Y = g(X,\theta)$, where $g$ is known in analytical form.
elements.sent-2411-076: In this model, $Y$ is observable with $X$ being latent.
elements.sent-2411-077: Our objective is to estimate the parameter $\theta$ based on the observed data $y:=\{Y_t\}_{t=1 ^T$.}
elements.sent-2411-078: In a special case where $X$ is one-dimensional with density $f(x)$, and $g$ is invertible with a differentiable inverse with respect to the $y$, a standard result in probability theory allows the density of $Y_t$ to be expressed in closed form as: $p(y;\theta) = f(g^{-1 (y;\theta))|\frac{d {dy g^{-1 (y;\theta)|.$ However, the theory developed in this paper does not require such restrictive assumptions.}}}}
elements.sent-2411-079: Instead, we only assume that $g$ is differentiable with respect to $x$ and that its gradient is non-zero a.e.
elements.sent-2411-080: Under this weaker condition, even though the analytical forms of $f$ and $g$ are known, the density of $Y$ may still be unknown.
elements.sent-2411-081: In this case, the likelihood function for $Y$ can only be expressed as: \begin{equation}\label{2} L_T(\theta) := \sum\limits_{t=1}\limits^{T}\log p(Y_t;\theta).\end{equation}.
elements.sent-2411-082: To maximize $L_{T (\theta)$, we compute the gradient of the log-likelihood: \begin{equation}\label{equation3} \nabla_{\theta}L_T(\theta) = \sum\limits_{t=1}\limits^{T}\frac{\nabla_{\theta}p(Y_t;\theta)}{p(Y_t;\theta)}.\end{equation}.}
elements.sent-2411-083: Suppose we have unbiased estimators for $\nabla_{\theta p(Y_t;\theta)$ and ${p(Y_t;\theta)}$ for every $\theta$ and $Y_t$.}
elements.sent-2411-084: While these individual estimators are unbiased, the ratio of two unbiased estimators may introduce bias.
elements.sent-2411-085: To distinguish between approaches, we refer to the previous algorithm using the plug-in estimator from Equation (\ref{single ) as the single time scale (STS) algorithm \citep{Peng2020 .}}
elements.sent-2411-086: To address this issue, we adopt an MTS framework that incorporates the gradient estimator into the iterative process, aiming for more accurate optimization results.
elements.sent-2411-087: Specifically, let $G_1(X,y,\theta)$ and $G_2(X,y,\theta)$ represent unbiased estimators obtained via Monte Carlo simulation: \begin{equation}\label{G1G2} G_1(X,Y_t,\theta) = \frac{1}{N}\sum_{i=1}^NG_1(X_i,Y_t,\theta),\quad G_2(X,Y_t,\theta) = \frac{1}{N}\sum_{i=1}^NG_2(X_i,Y_t,\theta),\end{equation} such that \begin{equation}E_X[G_1(X,Y_t,\theta)] = \nabla_{\theta}p(Y_t;\theta), \quad E_X[G_2(X,Y_t,\theta)] = p(Y_t;\theta).\end{equation}.
elements.sent-2411-088: The forms of $G_1$ and $G_2$ can be derived by GLR estimators \citep{Peng2020 .}
elements.sent-2411-089: Alternative single-run unbiased estimators for $G_1$ and $G_2$ can also be obtained via the conditional Monte Carlo method, as described in \citep{fu2009conditional .}
elements.sent-2411-090: We propose the iteration formulae for the MTS algorithm as follows: \begin{equation}\label{eq3} D_{k+1} = D_k + \alpha_k(G_{1,k}(X,Y,\theta_k)-G_{2,k}(X,Y,\theta_k)D_k),\end{equation} \begin{equation}\label{eq2} \theta_{k+1} = \Pi_{\Theta}(\theta_k + \beta_k E D_{k}),\end{equation} where $\Pi_{\Theta}$ is the projection operator that maps each iteratively obtained $\theta_k$ onto the feasible domain $\Theta$.
elements.sent-2411-091: The algebraic notations are as follows.
elements.sent-2411-092: That is to say, $G_{2,k (X,Y,\theta_k) = diag \{G_{2 (X,Y_1,\theta_k)I_d,\cdots,G_{2 (X,Y_T,\theta_k)I_d\} = diag \{G_{2 (X,Y_1,\theta_k),\cdots,G_{2 (X,Y_T,\theta_k)\}\otimes I_d$, which is a diagonal matrix with $T\times d$ rows and $T\times d$ columns.}}}}}
elements.sent-2411-093: The constant matrix $E=[I_d,I_d,\cdots,I_d] = e^T\otimes I_d$ is a block diagonal matrix with $d$ rows and $T\times d$ column, where $e$ is a column vector of ones.
elements.sent-2411-094: This matrix reshapes the long vector $D_k$ to match the structure of Equation (\ref{equation3 ), the summation of $T$ $d$-dimensional vectors.}
elements.sent-2411-095: In these two coupled iterations, $\theta_k$ is the parameter being optimized in the MLE process, as in Equation (\ref{single ).}
elements.sent-2411-096: The additional iteration for $D_k$ tracks the gradient of the log-likelihood function, mitigating ratio bias and numerical instability caused by denominator estimators.
elements.sent-2411-097: These two iterations operate on different time scales, with distinct update rates.
elements.sent-2411-098: Ideally, one would fix $\theta$, run iteration (\ref{eq3 ) until it converges to the true gradient, and then use this limit in iteration (\ref{eq2 ).}}
elements.sent-2411-099: However, such an approach is computationally inefficient.
elements.sent-2411-100: Instead, these coupled iterations are executed interactively, with iteration (\ref{eq3 ) running at a faster rate than (\ref{eq2 ), effectively treating $\theta$ as fixed in the second iteration.}}
elements.sent-2411-101: This time-scale separation is achieved by ensuring that the step sizes satisfy: $\frac{\beta_k {\alpha_k \rightarrow 0$ as $k$ tends to infinity.}}
elements.sent-2411-102: This design allows the gradient estimator's bias to average out over the iteration process, enabling accurate results even with a small Monte Carlo sample size $N$ in Equation (\ref{G1G2 ).}
elements.sent-2411-103: Ultimately, $E D_{k}$ converges to zero, and $\theta$ converges to its optimal value.
elements.sent-2411-104: The MTS framework for MLE is summarized as follows.
elements.algorithm-2411-001: \caption{(MTS for MLE)} \label{algor:1} \begin{algorithmic}[1] \State Input: data$\{Y_t\}_{t=1}^T$, initial iterative values $\theta_0$, $D_0$, number of samples $N$, iterative steps $K$, the step-sizes $\alpha_k$, $\beta_k$. \For {$k \text{ in } 0: K-1$} \State For $i=1:N$, sample $X_i$ and get unbiased estimators $G_{1,k}(X_i,Y,\theta_k)$, $G_{2,k}(X_i,Y,\theta_k)$. \State Do the iterations: \begin{equation*} \begin{aligned} D_{k+1} = D_k + \alpha_k(G_{1,k}(X,Y,\theta_k)-G_{2,k}(X,Y,\theta_k)D_k), \theta_{k+1} = \Pi_{\Theta}(\theta_k +\beta_k E D_{k}). \end{aligned} \end{equation*} \EndFor \State Output: $\theta_{K}$. \end{algorithmic}
elements.sent-2411-105: We now turn to the problem of estimating the posterior distribution of the parameter $\theta$ in the stochastic model $Y=g (X,\theta)$, where the analytical likelihood is unknown.
elements.sent-2411-106: The posterior distribution is defined as \begin{equation}p(\theta|y) = \frac{p(\theta)p(y|\theta)}{\int p(\theta)p(y|\theta)d\theta},\end{equation} where $p(\theta)$ is the known prior distribution, and $p(y|\theta)$ is the conditional density function that lacks an analytical form but can be estimated using an unbiased estimator.
elements.sent-2411-107: The denominator is a challenging normalization constant to handle and variational inference is a practical approach.
elements.sent-2411-108: In the variational inference framework, we approximate the posterior distribution $p(\theta|y)$ using a tractable density $q_{\lambda (\theta)$ with a variational parameter $\lambda$ to approximate.}
elements.sent-2411-109: The collection $\{q_{\lambda (\theta)\}$ is called the variational distribution family, and our goal is to find the optimal $\lambda$ by minimizing the KL divergence between tractable variational distribution $q_{\lambda (\theta)$ and the true posterior $p(\theta|y)$: \begin{equation}KL(\lambda) = KL(q_{\lambda}(\theta)\Vert p(\theta|y))=E_{q_{\lambda}(\theta)}[\log q_{\lambda}(\theta)-\log p(\theta|y)].\end{equation}.}}
elements.sent-2411-110: It is well known that minimizing KL divergence is equivalent to maximizing the ELBO, an expectation with respect to variational distribution $q_{\lambda (\theta)$: \begin{equation}L(\lambda) = \log p(y) - KL(\lambda) = E_{q_{\lambda}(\theta)}[\log p(y|\theta) + \log p(\theta) - \log q_{\lambda}(\theta)].\end{equation}.}
elements.formula-2411-011: \begin{equation}L(\lambda) = \log p(y) - KL(\lambda) = E_{q_{\lambda}(\theta)}[\log p(y|\theta) + \log p(\theta) - \log q_{\lambda}(\theta)].\end{equation}
elements.sent-2411-111: The problem is then reformulated as: \begin{equation}\lambda^{*} = \arg\max\limits_{\lambda \in \Lambda}{L(\lambda)},\end{equation} where $\Lambda$ is the feasible region of $\lambda$.
elements.sent-2411-112: It is essential to estimate the gradient of ELBO, which is an important problem in the field of machine learning and also falls under the umbrella of simulation optimization.
elements.sent-2411-113: Common methods for deriving gradient estimators include the score function method \citep{ranganath2014black and the re-parameterization trick \citep{kingma2013auto, rezende2014stochastic .}}
elements.sent-2411-114: In the simulation literature, these methods are also referred to as the likelihood ratio (LR) method and infinitesimal perturbation analysis (IPA) method, respectively \citep{FU2006575 .}
elements.sent-2411-116: However, in this paper, $p(y|\theta)$ is estimated by simulation rather than computed precisely, inducing bias to the $\log p(y|\theta)$ term.
elements.sent-2411-117: Furthermore, the score function method is prone to high variance \citep{rezende2014stochastic , making the re-parameterization trick a preferred choice.}
elements.sent-2411-118: Assume a variable substitution involving $\lambda$, such that $\theta = \theta(u;\lambda) \sim q_{\lambda (\theta)$, where $u$ is a random variable independent of $\lambda$ with density $p_0(u)$.}
elements.sent-2411-119: This represents a re-parameterization of $\theta$, where the stochastic component is incorporated into $u$, while the parameter $\lambda$ is isolated.

elements.section-2411-003: Asymptotic Analysis of GSPE Algorithm Framework
elements.sent-2411-143: In this section, we first derive the gradient estimator of the ELBO using the SAA method and analyze its asymptotic properties in Section \ref{sec3.1 .}
elements.sent-2411-144: The uniform convergence of the gradient estimator with respect to variational parameters plays a crucial role in ensuring the convergence of the two nested layers.
elements.sent-2411-145: For the optimization process, we outline the assumptions and notations in Section \ref{sec3.3 .}

elements.section-2411-004: Extension: Training Two Neural Networks at Different Time Scales
elements.sent-2411-312: In previous sections, we propose the GSPE algorithm framework and establish its asymptotic properties.
elements.sent-2411-313: The main idea involves using two coupled iterations to update parameters and eliminate ratio bias.
elements.sent-2411-314: Estimation and optimization are performed simultaneously through these coupled iterations: a faster iteration approximates the gradient of the log-likelihood function, while a slower iteration updates the variational parameter $\lambda$ in $q_{\lambda (\theta)$.}
elements.sent-2411-315: Additionally, the likelihood function and its gradient are estimated using unbiased estimators.
elements.sent-2411-316: However, when the simulator is sufficiently complex and unbiased estimators are challenging to obtain, more powerful tools are needed to approximate the likelihood function.
elements.sent-2411-317: Similarly, a more expressive variational distribution family $\{q_{\lambda (\theta)\}$ may be required to better represent the true posterior when it is complex.}
elements.sent-2411-318: To address the first challenge, a natural approach is to use a neural network to approximate the intractable likelihood function as an alternative to the GLR method \citep{papamakarios2019sequential .}
elements.sent-2411-319: The GLR method is advantageous due to its unbiasedness and simplicity but relies on relatively strict regularity conditions \citep{Peng2020 .}
elements.sent-2411-320: A neural network offers a flexible alternative when these conditions are not satisfied, though it provides a biased estimate of the likelihood function.
elements.sent-2411-321: Hence, we train a deep neural density estimator $p_{\phi (y|\theta)$ by minimizing the forward KL divergence between $p_{\phi (y|\theta)$ and the true conditional density $p(y|\theta)$, which is defined as \begin{equation}KL(p(y|\theta)\Vert p_{\phi}(y|\theta)) = \mathbb{E}_{\theta\sim q_{\lambda}(\theta),y\sim p(y|\theta)}\bigg[\log \bigg(\frac{p(y|\theta)}{p_{\phi}(y|\theta)}\bigg)\bigg].\end{equation}.}}
elements.sent-2411-322: This optimization minimizes the divergence between the unknown conditional density $p(y|\theta)$ and the network $p_{\phi (y|\theta)$ using samples $(\theta, y)$ generated from the simulator.}
elements.sent-2411-323: The loss function for the neural network at each iteration is: \begin{equation}L_{faster}(\phi) = -\frac{1}{MN}\sum_{m=1}^M\sum_{i=1}^N \log p_{\phi}(y_{m,i}|\theta_m), \quad \theta_m\sim q_{\lambda}(\theta),\ y_{m,i} \sim p(y|\theta_m),\end{equation} where $p_{\phi (y|\theta)$ acts as a conditional density estimator.}
elements.sent-2411-324: This network learns the true conditional density $p(y|\theta)$ by generating many samples from the simulator.
elements.sent-2411-325: While this process serves the same purpose as the GLR method—approximating the intractable likelihood—the estimation method differs.
elements.sent-2411-326: Here, $L_{faster}$ denotes that the neural network operates at a faster time scale, with a larger step-size.
elements.sent-2411-327: As in previous cases, while fixing $\lambda$ and iterating until convergence would provide accurate estimates, such an approach is computationally expensive.
elements.sent-2411-328: Thus, the coupled iterations are performed simultaneously, with the faster iteration preceding the slower one.
elements.sent-2411-329: To address the second challenge, another neural network $q_{\lambda (\theta)$ can be employed to construct a more expressive posterior distribution.}
elements.sent-2411-330: The loss function is the ELBO, as in Algorithm \ref{algor:2 : \begin{equation}L_{slower}(\lambda) = E_{q_{\lambda}(\theta)}[\log p_{\phi}(Y|\theta) + \log p(\theta) - \log q_{\lambda}(\theta)].\end{equation}.}
elements.sent-2411-331: Here $Y$ is the observed data and $p_{\phi (Y|\theta)$ is the likelihood network trained at the faster time scale.}
elements.sent-2411-332: Unlike Algorithm \ref{algor:2 , the convergence of $\phi$ is independent of the realization of $\theta$, so fixing the outer-layer samples is unnecessary.}
elements.sent-2411-333: The choice of the variational distribution family $q_{\lambda (\theta)$ is an important step.}
elements.sent-2411-334: Our MTS framework places no restrictions on the choice of the variational distribution family, which also implies its scalability and compatibility.
elements.sent-2411-335: Beyond simple choices such as the normal distribution, more sophisticated methods for selecting posterior distributions with good representational power have been studied.
elements.sent-2411-336: These include normalizing flows, such as planar flows, Masked Autoregressive Flow (MAF), Inverse Autoregressive Flow (IAF), and others \citep{pmlr-v37-rezende15, NIPS2017_6c1da886, dinh2017densityestimationusingreal, kingma2016improved .}
elements.sent-2411-337: Normalizing flows are powerful technique used to model complex probability distributions by mapping them from simpler, more tractable ones.
elements.sent-2411-338: This is achieved through a learned transformation, which acts as a bijective function.
elements.sent-2411-339: These flows are highly advantageous due to their flexibility in approximating a wide array of distribution shapes.
elements.sent-2411-340: Additionally, the re-parameterization trick is employed to ensure low-variance stochastic gradient estimation.
elements.sent-2411-341: Thus, there are two networks here.
elements.sent-2411-342: The faster scale network $p_{\phi (y|\theta)$ is used to update the parameters $\phi$ to track the intractable likelihood function $p(y|\theta)$, while the slower scale network $q_{\lambda (\theta)$ is used to approximate the posterior by updating the variational parameter $\lambda$.}}
elements.sent-2411-343: Optimization and estimation are alternately updated by two coupled neural networks, respectively.
elements.sent-2411-344: These are two coupled iterations with each updated at two different scales, which is contained in our MTS framework.
elements.sent-2411-345: The specific algorithm is given in Appendix \ref{appendix5.3 and numerical examples will be illustrated in Section \ref{sec5.3 .}}
elements.sent-2411-346: In Algorithm \ref{algor:3 , the neural network estimator introduces a bias compared to the likelihood function.}
elements.sent-2411-347: To account for this, Assumption \ref{assumption2 .4 is replaced by the following relaxed assumption: \begin{assumption $\mathbb{E [p_{\phi_k (Y_t|\theta) |\mathcal{F _k] -p(Y_t|\theta) = O(\gamma_k^{(1) )\rightarrow 0$, $\mathbb{E [\nabla_{\theta p_{\phi_k (Y_t|\theta) |\mathcal{F _k] -\nabla_{\theta p(Y_t|\theta) = O(\gamma_k^{(2) )\rightarrow 0$ for every $\theta$ and $t=1,2,\ldots,T$.}}}}}}}}}}}}
elements.sent-2411-348: These rates depend on the training settings and the network's properties, which may not be directly accessible.
elements.sent-2411-349: Under this assumption, the following proposition demonstrates that the shrinking bias at the faster time scale induces a corresponding bias reduction at the slower time scale.
elements.sent-2411-350: If $M$ is fixed, Assumptions \ref{assumption2 .1-\ref{assumption2 .3, \ref{assumption2 .5-\ref{assumption2 .8, \ref{assumption3 .1-\ref{assumption3 .2, and \ref{assum5 hold, the sequence ${\lambda_k}$ satisfies \begin{equation}\mathbb{E}[\Vert \lambda_k-\bar{\lambda}^M\Vert] = O(\sqrt{\beta_k}) + O(\sqrt{\gamma_k^{(1)}}) + O(\sqrt{\gamma_k^{(2)}}).\end{equation}.}}}}}}}

elements.subsection-2411-011: MTS for Training Likelihood and Posterior Neural Networks
elements.sent-2411-405: In this subsection, we employ neural networks to approximate likelihood functions and posteriors for more complicated models.
elements.sent-2411-406: In cases where the true posterior is unknown, direct comparisons between algorithms become challenging.
elements.sent-2411-407: Thus, Section \ref{sec5.3.1 illustrates the advantages of the MTS framework using a toy example, while Section \ref{sec5.3.2 describes its application to a complex simulator where analytical likelihood is infeasible.}}
elements.sent-2411-408: MAF method and IAF method \citep{NIPS2017_6c1da886,kingma2016improved are applied to build conditional likelihood estimator $p_{\phi (y|\theta)$ and variational distribution family $q_{\lambda (\theta)$, respectively based on their specific nature.}}}
elements.sent-2411-409: Details of the MAF and IAF setups are provided in Appendix \ref{appendixE.2 .}
elements.sent-2411-410: The results demonstrate the superior accuracy of the MTS algorithm compared to the corresponding STS algorithm.
elements.sent-2411-411: Figure \ref{ex3 shows that the posterior estimated by MTS closely matches the true posterior, whereas the posterior estimated by STS exhibits noticeable deviation.}
elements.sent-2411-412: Notably, MTS achieves this improvement without additional computational burden, as the primary adjustment lies in the training speeds of the two neural networks.

elements.subsubsection-2411-002: Parameter Estimation in Food Preparation Process
elements.sent-2411-413: Here $Y$ is the output, $X$ characterizes the stochasticity of the model, and $\theta$ comprises the parameters whose posterior distribution we aim to estimate.
elements.sent-2411-414: In this case, the analytical likelihood $p(Y|\theta)$ is absent and the joint posterior of parameters could be complex.
elements.sent-2411-415: We need a general variational parameter class, a neural network, to represent the posterior better rather than a normal distribution with only two variational parameters in Section \ref{sec5.2 .}
elements.sent-2411-416: First, we introduce the setting of the simulator.
elements.sent-2411-417: Assume that order arrival follows a Poisson distribution with parameter 2.
elements.sent-2411-418: The food preparation process comprises three stages.
elements.sent-2411-419: At first, one clerk is checking and processing the order, and the processing time follows a Gamma distribution with shape parameter 3 and inverse scale parameter 2.
elements.sent-2411-420: Next, three cooks are preparing the food, where the preparation time is the first parameter $\theta_1$ whose posterior we want to estimate.
elements.sent-2411-421: After the food is prepared, one clerk is responsible for packing the food and the packing time is the second parameter $\theta_2$ we want to estimate.
elements.sent-2411-422: Each procedure can be modeled as a single server or three servers queue with a buffer of unlimited capacity, where each job is served based on the first-in/first-out discipline.
elements.sent-2411-423: The final observation is the time series of the completion time of the food orders.
elements.sent-2411-424: This process is illustrated in Figure \ref{figure9 in Appendix \ref{appendixE.2 .}}
elements.sent-2411-425: To obtain the observations, we sample $\theta = (\theta_1, \theta_2)$ ten times from independent Gamma distribution $(\Gamma(4,2), \Gamma(1,1))$.
elements.sent-2411-426: Then by realizing the stochastic part $X$ and plugging them into the model, we can obtain a realization of the 10-dimensional output $\hat{Y (X,\theta)$ as our observation.}
elements.sent-2411-427: The posterior is estimated based on this observation.
elements.sent-2411-428: The prior of $\theta$ is set to be a uniform distribution: $\theta \sim \mathcal{U (0,15)$.}
elements.sent-2411-429: MAF and IAF methods are also applied to build $p_{\phi (y|\theta)$ and $q_{\lambda (\theta)$ in setting the same as Section \ref{sec5.3.1 .}}}
elements.sent-2411-430: The details for training can be found in Appendix \ref{appendixE.2 .}
elements.sent-2411-431: Figure \ref{ex3_2}\subref{ex3_2_1 demonstrates the posterior estimated by MTS, with the light blue region on the edge representing the marginal distribution.}
elements.sent-2411-432: Due to the complexity of the joint density, employing a neural network as a general variational class is necessary.
elements.sent-2411-433: For the output performance measure, we generate another output using parameters sampled from the posterior.
elements.sent-2411-434: Figure \ref{ex3_2}\subref{ex3_2_2 illustrates that the resulting sequence closely matches the original observations, despite the prior being far from the posterior.}
elements.figure-2411-009: \caption{Posterior estimated by MTS through neural networks} \label{ex3_2} \subfigure[Density Plot with Marginal Distributions]{ \includegraphics[width=6.5cm]{fig5_2_MTS.pdf} \label{ex3_2_1} } \subfigure[Output performance]{ \includegraphics[width=6.5cm]{fig5_2_output.pdf} \label{ex3_2_2} }
elements.sent-2411-435: A similar conclusion can be verified in our MTS method.
elements.sent-2411-436: Input fitting refers to the case that the input data is observable and we can get the estimation of $\theta$ in input distribution directly.
elements.sent-2411-437: Output fitting is our problem setting, where we only have the final output $Y$.
elements.sent-2411-438: In the case of model misspecification, we generate the observations from the model where the first procedure follows a Gamma distribution $\Gamma(5,1.25)$ with shape parameter 5 and inverse scale parameter $1.25$ rather than $\Gamma(3,2)$ as before.
elements.sent-2411-439: On the other hand, we calculate the MTS based on the model we assumed before, which means the data mismatches with the model.
elements.sent-2411-440: Figure \ref{ex3_3}\subref{ex3_3_1 depicts the posterior estimated in such a situation.}
elements.sent-2411-441: According to Figure \ref{ex3_3}\subref{ex3_3_2 , MTS performs better than input fitting in terms of the accuracy of output performance measures.}
elements.sent-2411-442: The completion time series of the misspecified model with the parameter $\theta$ fitted by MTS is much closer to the completion time series of the true model than the completion time series of the misspecified model fitted by the input data.
elements.sent-2411-443: Therefore, it could be beneficial to fit the output data rather than the input data if the model is potentially misspecified, which makes our methods come in handy.
elements.figure-2411-010: \caption{Posterior estimated by MTS under model misspecification} \label{ex3_3} \subfigure[Bivariate Density Plot with Marginal Distributions under model misspecification]{ \includegraphics[width=6.5cm]{ex5_3_missp.pdf} \label{ex3_3_1} } \subfigure[Output performance under input fitting and output fitting]{ \includegraphics[width=6.5cm]{output_fitting.pdf} \label{ex3_3_2} }
elements.sent-2411-444: This paper presents a comprehensive study addressing the challenges of parameter estimation where the likelihood function is estimated by simulations.
elements.sent-2411-445: Our GSPE approach, grounded in the MTS algorithm, handles the ratio bias problem, enhances the accuracy of parameter estimation, and saves computational costs.
elements.sent-2411-446: We have demonstrated that our method not only achieves strong convergence and $\mathbb{L ^1$ convergence but also admits to a CLT, providing a solid theoretical foundation for practical applications.}
elements.sent-2411-447: In the realm of PDE, we have explored a nested simulation optimization structure, which is both theoretically sound and empirically effective.
elements.sent-2411-448: Furthermore, we have introduced neural network training to our model, showcasing the versatility and scalability of our framework.
elements.sent-2411-449: Future work encompasses eliminating ratio bias in more scenarios, and our framework can be more widely applied and extended.. \begin{appendices.}

elements.sent-2411-491: Next, we proceed to prove the main part of the convergence theory.

elements.sent-2411-494: First, we construct the corresponding step interpolation functions $\{D_m^k(t),\lambda^k(t)\}$ for the sequence.
elements.sent-2411-495: Then, we demonstrate that these functions $\{D_m^k(t),\lambda^k(t)\}$ converge to a solution of the ODE as the number of iterations becomes sufficiently large.
elements.sent-2411-496: The asymptotic stability point of this ODE corresponds to the limiting point of the iterative sequence $\{D_{k,m ,\theta_k\}$.}
elements.sent-2411-497: Finally, we show that the condition satisfied by this convergence point is $D=0$, $\lambda= \bar{\lambda ^M$.}

elements.sent-2411-656: In this part, we describe the methodologies employed to estimate the conditional density $p_{\phi (y|\theta)$ using an MAF network and to approximate the posterior $q_{\lambda (\theta)$ using an IAF network.}}
elements.sent-2411-657: Both networks utilize a similar architecture based on autoregressive models, leveraging their distinct advantages for density estimation and sampling.
elements.sent-2411-658: Autoregressive models facilitate the modeling of complex distributions by ensuring that each output feature depends solely on its preceding features.
elements.sent-2411-659: This is achieved through a masking mechanism called Masked Autoencoder for Distribution Estimation (MADE), as detailed in \cite{germain2015made .}
elements.sent-2411-660: Figure \ref{app1 illustrates the forward MAF algorithm workflow with a single MADE layer.}
elements.sent-2411-661: Our constructed MAF network consists of 5 MADE layers, with each MADE layer containing 3 hidden layers and 50 neurons per hidden layer.
elements.sent-2411-662: Each MADE layer produces a series of mean $m_i$ and scale parameters $e^{s_i}$ by training on simulated data $y$ and $\theta$.
elements.sent-2411-663: These parameters enable the transformation of the target distribution into a base distribution, typically a standard normal distribution, through an invertible transformation $u=T(y)$.
elements.sent-2411-664: Note that $m_i$ and $s_i$ are only determined by $\theta$ and $y_{1:i-1}$ due to the autoregressive model in MADE, so $u_i$ can be calculated in parallel by formula $u_i=(y_i-m_i)e^{-s_i}$.
elements.sent-2411-665: Furthermore, the calculation of conditional density requires the Jacobian determinant: $\log p(y|\theta) = \log p_{u (u) + \log|det(\frac{\partial T {\partial y )|.$ Since this Jacobian matrix is lower diagonal, hence determinant can be computed efficiently, which ensures that we can efficiently calculate conditional density $p_{\phi (y|\theta)$ by plugging the value of $u$ and Jacobian determinant.}}}}
elements.sent-2411-666: On the other hand, the IAF network mirrors the architecture of the MAF in Figure \ref{app1 , which serves as a variational distribution to model the posterior $q_{\lambda (\theta)$.}}
elements.sent-2411-667: It also employs an autoregressive structure, which allows for effective sampling from the approximate posterior.
elements.sent-2411-668: Our IAF network comprises 5 autoregressive layers with 3 hidden layers and 11 neurons per hidden layer.
elements.sent-2411-669: The IAF network generates an invertible transformation that facilitates mapping from a base distribution to the approximate posterior distribution: $y_i = u_i\exp(s_i) + m_i$.
elements.sent-2411-670: Here $s_i$ and $m_i$ are determined by $u_{1:i-1}$, which makes it calculated parallelly.
elements.sent-2411-671: Therefore, IAF is particularly effective for sampling $\theta$ from its posterior.
elements.sent-2411-672: In our experiment, after constructing the above two neural networks, we set up the training parameters as below.
elements.sent-2411-673: The learning rate for the faster scale is $\alpha_k = 10^{-3}$.
elements.sent-2411-674: While the learning rate for the slower scale is $\beta_k = 0.996^k\times10^{-3}$, satisfying the MTS condition $\beta_k/\alpha_k\rightarrow 0$.
elements.sent-2411-675: In every iteration, we simulate $M = 10^3$ outer layer samples and $N=1$ inner layer samples to train the two networks.
elements.sent-2411-676: After 10 rounds of coupled iterations, we can get the posterior of $\theta$ based on this sequence of observation $\hat{Y (X,\theta)$.}
elements.sent-2411-677: The process in Section \ref{sec5.3.2 is illustrated as follows.}
elements.algorithm-2411-003: \caption{(MTS for training likelihood and posterior neural networks)} \label{algor:3} \begin{algorithmic}[1] \State Input: data $Y$:$\{Y_t\}_{t=1}^T$, prior $p(\theta)$, iteration rounds $K$, number of outer layer samples and inter layer samples: $M$, $N$. \For {$k \text{ in } 0: K-1$} \State Simulate $\theta_{m}$ from $q_{\lambda_k}(\theta)$ for $m=1:M$; \State Sample $\{X_{m,i}\}$ and calculate the corresponding output $y_{m,i} = g(X_{m,i},\theta_{m})$ for $i=1:N$ and $m=1:M$; \State Train $p_{\phi_k}(y|\theta)$ with a faster speed: $\phi_{k+1} = \arg\min_{\phi} -\frac{1}{MN}\sum_{m,i} \log p_{\phi}(y_{m,i}|\theta_{m}).$ \State Train $q_{\lambda_k}(\theta)$ with a slower speed: $\lambda_{k+1} = \arg\max_{\lambda}\mathbb{E}_{q_{\lambda}(\theta)}[\log p_{\phi_k}(Y|\theta) + \log p(\theta) - \log q_{\lambda}(\theta)].$ \EndFor \State Output: posterior $q_{\lambda_{K}}(\theta)$. \end{algorithmic}
elements.sent-2411-678: We study parameter inference in simulation-based stochastic models where the analytical form of the likelihood is unknown.
elements.sent-2411-679: The main difficulty is that score evaluation as a ratio of noisy Monte Carlo estimators induces bias and instability, which we overcome with a ratio-free nested multi-time-scale (NMTS) stochastic approximation (SA) method that simultaneously tracks the score and drives the parameter update.
elements.sent-2411-680: We provide a comprehensive theoretical analysis of the proposed NMTS algorithm for solving likelihood-free inference problems, including strong convergence, asymptotic normality, and convergence rates.
elements.sent-2411-681: We show that our algorithm can eliminate the original asymptotic bias $O\big(\sqrt{\frac{1 {N }\big)$ and accelerate the convergence rate from $O\big(\beta_k+\sqrt{\frac{1 {N }\big)$ to $O\big(\frac{\beta_k {\alpha_k +\sqrt{\frac{\alpha_k {N }\big)$, where $N$ is the fixed batch size, $\alpha_k$ and $\beta_k$ are decreasing step sizes with $\alpha_k$, $\beta_k$, $\beta_k/\alpha_k\rightarrow 0$.}}}}}}}}
elements.sent-2411-682: With proper choice of $\alpha_k$ and $\beta_k$, our convergence rates can match the optimal rate in the multi-time-scale SA literature.
elements.sent-2411-683: Numerical experiments demonstrate that our algorithm can improve the estimation accuracy by one to two orders of magnitude at the same computational cost, making it efficient for parameter estimation in stochastic systems.
elements.sent-2411-684: In statistical inference, likelihood-free parameter estimation (LFPE) refers to the case where the analytical form of the likelihood function is not available, and how to infer the model parameters based on observed data becomes difficult.
elements.sent-2411-685: Key inference methods include maximum likelihood estimation (MLE), which obtains a point estimate by maximizing the likelihood, and posterior density estimation (PDE), which views inference as density approximation and typically uses variational inference to select the distribution closest to the target posterior within a chosen variational family.
elements.sent-2411-686: Traditional density estimation focuses on approximating an unknown distribution $p_{\text{data (x)$ with a model $p_{\theta (x)$ parameterized by $\theta$.}}}
elements.sent-2411-687: Depending on whether the likelihood is tractable, existing approaches include explicit likelihood models such as exponential families and normalizing flows \citep{papamakarios2021normalizing , unnormalized energy models trained via score matching or Stein operators \citep{hyvarinen2005estimation,anastasiou2023stein , and implicit generative models such as GANs and diffusion models \citep{puchkin2024rates .}}}
elements.sent-2411-688: Despite methodological diversity, these approaches share a common goal: learning a density over data.
elements.sent-2411-689: In contrast, this paper addresses a different class of inference problems, where the target of learning is not the density of data itself, but the parameters of a stochastic system that implicitly generates observable data.
elements.sent-2411-690: Examples include system dynamics models, complex network models, and Lindley's recursion in queueing models.
elements.sent-2411-691: In such systems, the likelihood function of the observed data $p(Y;\theta)$ is intractable, posing a major obstacle for parameter calibration and statistical inference \citep{gross2011fundamentals, shepherd2014review,Peng2020 .}
elements.sent-2411-692: Estimating or optimizing parameters in this setting requires differentiating the implicit likelihood with respect to $\theta$, leading to the score function $\nabla_{\theta}\log p(y;\theta)$.
elements.sent-2411-693: However, when only Monte Carlo estimators of $\nabla_{\theta p(y;\theta)$ and $p(y;\theta)$ are available, the ratio $\nabla_{\theta p(y;\theta)/p(y;\theta)$ introduces a systematic ratio bias that hampers convergence and stability.}}
elements.sent-2411-694: Existing studies have investigated similar ratio-estimation problems from different perspectives.
elements.sent-2411-695: For instance, density-ratio estimation methods directly learn $q/p$ from samples drawn from two data distributions \citep{sugiyama2010least,sugiyama2012density,sugiyama2012density2,thomas2022likelihood , and score-based or density-derivative-ratio estimators learn normalized derivatives like $\nabla_x p(x)/p(x)$ to characterize data-space geometry \citep{sasaki2017estimating,sasaki2018mode .}}
elements.sent-2411-696: These approaches rely on access to data-space gradients or two well-defined distributions in $x$, and the ratio is computed analytically within a deterministic objective.
elements.sent-2411-697: In contrast, our ratio $\nabla_\theta p_\theta(y)/p_\theta(y)$ resides in the parameter domain, and both numerator and denominator are obtained through Monte Carlo simulation.
elements.sent-2411-698: Consequently, existing ratio-estimation techniques cannot be applied, since their unbiasedness and convergence rely on exact evaluations or deterministic gradients, whereas in our case, the two components are stochastic and correlated, making the direct division intrinsically biased.
elements.sent-2411-699: This motivates our development of a ratio-free nested multi-time-scale (NMTS) stochastic approximation (SA) scheme that recursively tracks the desired parameter-space score without explicit division.
elements.sent-2411-700: Therefore, we cast the inference problem as a stochastic optimization task, jointly performing estimation and optimization \citep{Kushner1997StochasticAA,borkar2009stochastic .}
elements.sent-2411-701: The core idea is to treat both the parameters and the gradient of the logarithmic likelihood function jointly as components of a stochastic root-finding problem for a coupled system of nonlinear equations.
elements.sent-2411-702: This approach attempts to approximate the solution by devising two separate but coupled iterations, where one component is updated at a faster pace than the other.
elements.sent-2411-703: This method continually refines the gradient estimator by averaging all available simulation data, thereby eliminating ratio bias throughout the iterative process.
elements.sent-2411-704: Building on the rich SA literature on the vanilla MTS methods \citep{borkar2009stochastic,Kushner1997StochasticAA,doan2022nonlinear,hu2022stochastic,hong2023two,hu2024quantile,zeng2024two,lin2025two,cao2025black,cao2024kernel , which provides a versatile and well-understood foundation, we develop an NMTS scheme expressly tailored to the structural demands of our likelihood-free inference problem.}
elements.sent-2411-705: In our setting, variational inference is used to perform density estimation by maximizing the evidence lower bound (ELBO) \citep{blei2017variational , whose unbiased gradient estimator appears as an expectation involving a likelihood ratio.}
elements.sent-2411-706: To evaluate this outer expectation, we first adopt the sample average approximation (SAA) to draw a finite set of outer samples.
elements.sent-2411-707: Then, for each outer sample, we design a set of parallel fast-timescale recursions that update local estimates of the likelihood-ratio gradient.
elements.sent-2411-708: These parallel recursions are subsequently coupled into the slow-timescale parameter update through a weighted averaging mechanism.
elements.sent-2411-709: This structure allows the fast-timescale to track the local ratio estimates, while the inner recursions continuously update the parameters by the overall ELBO gradient represented by the combination of parallel fast-timescale recursions.
elements.sent-2411-710: In this way, we effectively extend the classical multi-time-scale SA to a nested and parallel setting, specifically adapted to intractable likelihood simulators.
elements.sent-2411-711: We also provide a comprehensive theoretical analysis of the proposed NMTS algorithm, including almost-sure convergence, asymptotic normality, and the $\mathbb{L ^1$ convergence rate.}
elements.sent-2411-712: The main technical challenge arises from the nested structure of the algorithm, where the inner recursions evolve on different timescales while the outer recursion depends on an SAA-based approximation of the ELBO gradient.
elements.sent-2411-713: In the strong convergence analysis, the introduction of outer samples requires proving that the SAA gradient estimator remains uniformly consistent when the inner parameter $\lambda_k$ changes during the iteration, a result guaranteed by Donsker's Theorem in Proposition \ref{proposition1 .}
elements.sent-2411-714: Theorems \ref{thm1 and \ref{thm2 respectively establish the uniform convergence of the parallel fast-timescale recursions and the convergence of the slow-timescale updates.}}
elements.sent-2411-715: Our proof constructs a sequence of continuous time interpolations of the discrete iterates and shows that they are asymptotically governed by a limiting system of ordinary differential equations (ODEs).
elements.sent-2411-716: The stationary point of this ODE system coincides with the almost sure limit of the NMTS iterates, thereby extending convergence results in prior work to the nested and high-dimensional setting considered here.
elements.sent-2411-717: Proposition \ref{proposition3 and \ref{proposition4 further establish that the convergence results hold for the two-layer structure of the algorithm, extending the previous pioneering stochastic approximation literature \citep{doan2022nonlinear,hong2023two,zeng2024two,lin2025two,hu2022stochastic,hu2024quantile,cao2025black .}}}
elements.sent-2411-718: For the weak convergence results, we build upon the framework in \cite{mokkadem2006convergence to analyze the weak limits of both the inner and outer stochastic processes.}
elements.sent-2411-719: Theorem \ref{thm6 characterizes the weak convergence of the NMTS iterates, while Theorems \ref{theorem5 and \ref{theorem6 provide the weak convergence and corresponding rates for the outer-layer SAA sequence.}}}
elements.sent-2411-720: Finally, the $\mathbb{L ^1$ convergence rate analysis in Theorem \ref{thm5 shows that, for a fixed batch size $N$, the NMTS algorithm achieves $O(\frac{\beta_k {\alpha_k +\sqrt{\frac{\alpha_k {N )$, where $\alpha_k$ and $\beta_k$ are decreasing step sizes satisfying $\beta_k$, $\alpha_k$, $\beta_k/\alpha_k\to0$.}}}}}}}
elements.sent-2411-721: With an appropriate choice of step-size schedule, the optimal $\mathbb{L ^1$ rate is $O(k^{-1/3 )$, which coincides with the convergence rate in the literature \citep{doan2022nonlinear,hong2023two .}}}
elements.sent-2411-722: In contrast, conventional single-timescale algorithms suffer from a persistent asymptotic bias of order $O(\sqrt{1/N )$ due to the ratio bias in gradient estimation.}
elements.sent-2411-723: Our NMTS framework removes this bias through the coupled fast-timescale recursive estimator, thereby yielding a provably faster and consistent convergence behavior.
elements.sent-2411-724: Furthermore, we introduce the concept of different timescales into neural network training to demonstrate the compatibility and scalability of our NMTS framework.
elements.sent-2411-725: For overly complex simulators, we use a neural network as an alternative to estimate the intractable likelihood.
elements.sent-2411-726: Additionally, when the posterior is complex and the simple variational distribution family has limited representational ability, another neural network can serve as the variational distribution.
elements.sent-2411-727: We design an NMTS algorithm that adjusts the update frequency of the two neural networks to ensure convergence and improve training outcomes.
elements.sent-2411-728: Our approach provides theoretical support for such estimation and optimization algorithms that require updates at different frequencies.
elements.sent-2411-730: We introduce a new NMTS algorithm that jointly addresses MLE and PDE problems when the likelihood function is intractable.
elements.sent-2411-731: The new NMTS SA scheme transforms the ratio estimator into a coupled root-finding system with two interacting time scales.
elements.sent-2411-732: The fast recursions track local score estimates through parallel updates, while the slow recursion aggregates these estimates to optimize the ELBO or likelihood objective.
elements.sent-2411-733: This structure extends classical multi-time-scale SA theory to a nested and parallel regime tailored to likelihood-free simulators.
elements.sent-2411-734: Theoretical guarantees for nested stochastic optimization.
elements.sent-2411-735: We establish strong convergence, weak convergence, and $\mathbb{L ^1$ convergence rate results for the NMTS algorithm, proving that the algorithm converges almost surely to the true solution of the intractable likelihood inference problem.}
elements.sent-2411-736: The analysis introduces new techniques to ensure uniform convergence of SAA-based gradient estimators along the entire algorithmic trajectory—an essential step absent in prior SA literature—and characterizes the joint asymptotic behavior of both layers.
elements.sent-2411-737: Eliminating asymptotic bias and better empirical results.
elements.sent-2411-738: Theoretically, our NMTS algorithm removes the ratio-induced asymptotic bias $O(\sqrt{1/N )$ that persists in single-timescale methods and achieves a faster $\mathbb{L ^1$ convergence rate of $O\!\left(\frac{\beta_k {\alpha_k +\sqrt{\frac{\alpha_k {N }\right)$, with an optimal decay rate of $O(k^{-1/3 )$ under proper step-size scheduling.}}}}}}}
elements.sent-2411-739: This result significantly sharpens the asymptotic efficiency bounds for simulation-based inference.
elements.sent-2411-740: Consistent with the theory, our experiments show that NMTS delivers lower error than STS under matched computational budgets.
elements.sent-2411-741: The remainder of the paper is organized as follows.
elements.sent-2411-743: Section \ref{section2 provides the necessary background and introduces the NMTS algorithm for both MLE and PDE cases.}
elements.sent-2411-744: In Section \ref{sec3 , we conduct an in-depth analysis of the algorithm, establishing consistency results and convergence rates.}
elements.sent-2411-745: Section \ref{sec4 extends the NMTS framework to neural network training.}
elements.sent-2411-746: The existing literature related to our topic can be organized into two main parts: likelihood-free parameter estimation and the MTS algorithm.. Likelihood-free parameter estimation.
elements.sent-2411-747: The LFPE problem is closely related to the simulation-based inference problem in the literature.

elements.sent-2411-848: The following proposition connects the inner recursion and the outer approximation.
elements.sent-2411-849: It shows that the recursive estimator $S_k^M$, which aggregates the outputs of the fast-timescale updates, asymptotically tracks the true gradient of the ELBO as both the iteration number and the number of outer samples grow.
elements.sent-2411-850: This result bridges the SA dynamics of the algorithm with the statistical consistency for SAA.
elements.sent-2411-851: Proposition~\ref{proposition3 thus ensures that the fast-timescale recursion delivers an asymptotically unbiased estimate of the true ELBO gradient, which is essential for guaranteeing the correctness of the subsequent slow-timescale parameter updates.}
elements.sent-2411-852: The next proposition establishes the final layer of convergence.
elements.sent-2411-853: It proves that the stationary point of the approximate optimization problem based on $M$ outer samples converges to the true optimum as $M$ increases, thereby closing the loop of the nested convergence analysis.
elements.sent-2411-854: Proposition~\ref{proposition4 formally guarantees that the nested simulation optimization algorithm is statistically consistent: the limit of the algorithmic iterates coincides with the true maximum of the expected ELBO as the number of outer samples tends to infinity.}
elements.sent-2411-855: Having established strong convergence in the previous subsection, we now turn to the characterization of the limiting distribution and convergence rate of the NMTS algorithm.
elements.sent-2411-856: While strong convergence guarantees that the iterates $\{\lambda_k\}$ and $\{D_k\}$ asymptotically approach their deterministic limits, it does not quantify how the stochastic noise introduced by finite samples propagates through the coupled recursions.
elements.sent-2411-857: For example, the fixed sample size $N$ used in the estimation of $G_1$ and $G_2$ in each iteration controls the variance of the stochastic gradients.
elements.sent-2411-858: To address this, we study the weak convergence and asymptotic normality of the NMTS iterates, which describe their second-order stochastic behavior and allow us to derive convergence rates in distribution.
elements.sent-2411-859: The weak convergence analysis requires additional regularity on the curvature of the objective and on the step-size sequences.
elements.sent-2411-860: The following assumption, standard in SA literature \citep{bottou2018optimization, hu2024quantile,cao2025black , ensures that the algorithm operates within a locally stable regime where the limiting distribution exists and is asymptotically normal.}
elements.sent-2411-861: This result formalizes the idea that, after appropriate rescaling, the deviations of $\lambda_k$ and $D_k$ around their equilibrium points converge in distribution to independent Gaussian limits.
elements.sent-2411-862: Their covariance matrices characterize the asymptotic variability of the algorithm induced by stochastic gradient noise at the corresponding time scales.

elements.sent-2411-984: Notice the definition of $\mathcal{F _k$, take the conditional expectation on both sides, we can get \begin{equation}\label{eq7} \begin{split} \mathbb{E}[\Vert D_{k+1,m}\Vert ^2|\mathcal{F}_k] \\\le \mathbb{E}[\Vert I-\alpha_k G_{2,k,m}\Vert ^2|\mathcal{F}_k]\cdot\Vert D_{k,m}\Vert ^2 + 2\alpha_k \mathbb{E}[\Vert I-\alpha_kG_{2,k,m}\Vert \cdot\Vert G_{1,k,m}\Vert |\mathcal{F}_k]\cdot\Vert D_{k,m}\Vert + \alpha_k^2\mathbb{E}[\Vert G_{1,k,m}\Vert ^2|\mathcal{F}] \\\le \mathbb{E}[\Vert I-\alpha_k G_{2,k,m}\Vert ^2|\mathcal{F}_k]\Vert D_{k,m}\Vert ^2 + 2\alpha_k \sqrt{\mathbb{E}[\Vert G_{1,k,m}\Vert ^2|\mathcal{F}_k]} \sqrt{\mathbb{E}[\Vert I-\alpha_kG_{2,k,m}\Vert ^2 |\mathcal{F}_k]}\Vert D_{k,m}\Vert +\alpha_k^2 C_1. \end{split}\end{equation} The second inequality comes from Cauchy-Schwarz(C-S) inequality and Assumption \ref{assumption2 .1.}}
elements.formula-2411-152: \begin{equation}\label{eq7}  E[\Vert D_{k+1,m}\Vert ^2|F_k] \\\le E[\Vert I-\alpha_k G_{2,k,m}\Vert ^2|F_k]\cdot\Vert D_{k,m}\Vert ^2 + 2\alpha_k E[\Vert I-\alpha_kG_{2,k,m}\Vert \cdot\Vert G_{1,k,m}\Vert |F_k]\cdot\Vert D_{k,m}\Vert + \alpha_k^2E[\Vert G_{1,k,m}\Vert ^2|F] \\\le E[\Vert I-\alpha_k G_{2,k,m}\Vert ^2|F_k]\Vert D_{k,m}\Vert ^2 + 2\alpha_k \sqrt{E[\Vert G_{1,k,m}\Vert ^2|F_k]} \sqrt{E[\Vert I-\alpha_kG_{2,k,m}\Vert ^2 |F_k]}\Vert D_{k,m}\Vert +\alpha_k^2 C_1. \end{equation}
elements.sent-2411-985: Note that \begin{equation} \mathbb{E}[\Vert I-\alpha_k G_{2,k,m}\Vert ^2|\mathcal{F}_k] = \mathbb{E}[(1-\alpha_k \lambda_{2,k,m})^2|\mathcal{F}_k] = 1-\alpha_k(2\mathbb{E}[\lambda_{2,k,m}|\mathcal{F}_k]-\alpha_k\mathbb{E}[\lambda_{2,k,m}^2|\mathcal{F}_k])\le 1-\alpha_k \epsilon,\end{equation} where $\lambda_{2,k,m}$ is the minimum eigenvalue of $G_{2,k,m}$.
elements.sent-2411-986: Take the expectation and apply the C-S inequality, the inequality holds for every $k\ge N_1$, \begin{equation}E[\Vert D_{k+1,m}\Vert ^2] \le(1-\alpha_k\epsilon)E[\Vert D_{k,m}\Vert ^2] + \alpha_k^2C_1+2\alpha_k \sqrt{C_1}\sqrt{1-\alpha_k \epsilon}\sqrt{E[\Vert D_{k,m}\Vert ^2]} = \bigg(\sqrt{1-\alpha_k \epsilon}\sqrt{E[\Vert D_{k,m}\Vert ^2]} + \alpha_k \sqrt{C_1} \bigg)^2 \le \bigg((1-\frac{\alpha_k\epsilon}{2})\sqrt{E[\Vert D_{k,m}\Vert ^2]} + \frac{\alpha_k\epsilon}{2}\frac{2\sqrt{C_1}}{\epsilon}\bigg)^2 \le \bigg(\max_k\{\sqrt{E[\Vert D_{k,m}\Vert ^2]},\frac{2\sqrt{C_1}}{\epsilon}\}\bigg)^2.\end{equation}.
elements.formula-2411-153: \begin{equation}E[\Vert D_{k+1,m}\Vert ^2] \le(1-\alpha_k\epsilon)E[\Vert D_{k,m}\Vert ^2] + \alpha_k^2C_1+2\alpha_k \sqrt{C_1}\sqrt{1-\alpha_k \epsilon}\sqrt{E[\Vert D_{k,m}\Vert ^2]} = \bigg(\sqrt{1-\alpha_k \epsilon}\sqrt{E[\Vert D_{k,m}\Vert ^2]} + \alpha_k \sqrt{C_1} \bigg)^2 \le \bigg((1-\frac{\alpha_k\epsilon}{2})\sqrt{E[\Vert D_{k,m}\Vert ^2]} + \frac{\alpha_k\epsilon}{2}\frac{2\sqrt{C_1}}{\epsilon}\bigg)^2 \le \bigg(\max_k\{\sqrt{E[\Vert D_{k,m}\Vert ^2]},\frac{2\sqrt{C_1}}{\epsilon}\}\bigg)^2.\end{equation}
elements.sent-2411-987: Since $D_0$ is independent of $u$, by using the boundness assumption, taking the expectation and taking superior with respect to $m$ in Equation (\ref{eq7 ), it is easy to prove by induction that for every $k\le N_1$, $\sup_{m}\mathbb{E [\Vert D_{k,m}\Vert ^2] < \infty$.}}
elements.sent-2411-989: Rewrite the iteration as \begin{equation}\label{eq8} \begin{split} D_{k+1,m} = (I-\alpha_k G_{2,k,m})D_{k,m} + \alpha_k G_{1,k,m} = (I- \alpha_k \mathbb{E}[G_{2,k,m}|\mathcal{F}_k])D_{k,m} + \alpha_k\mathbb{E}[G_{1,k,m}|\mathcal{F}_k] + \alpha_k W_{k,m} + \alpha_k V_{k,m} = (I-U_{k,m})D_{k,m} + \tilde{\alpha}_kR_{k,m} + \alpha_kW_{k,m} + \alpha_k V_{k,m}, \end{split}\end{equation} where $W_{k,m = (\mathbb{E [G_{2,k,m |\mathcal{F _k]-G_{2,k,m )D_{k,m}$, $V_{k,m = G_{1,k,m -\mathbb{E [G_{1,k,m |\mathcal{F _k], $ $U_{k,m =\alpha_k \mathbb{E [G_{2,k,m |\mathcal{F _k],$ $R_{k,m = \mathbb{E [G_{2,k,m |\mathcal{F _k]^{-1}\mathbb{E [G_{1,k,m |\mathcal{F _k]$.}}}}}}}}}}}}}}}}}}}}}

elements.sent-2411-990: We have the inequality: $\prod\limits_{i=N_2 ^{k (1-\tilde{\alpha _i)\Vert D_{N_2,m}\Vert \le e^{-\sum_{i=N_2 ^k\tilde{\alpha _i}\Vert D_{N_2,m}\Vert \le e^{-\epsilon \sum_{i=N_2 ^k\alpha_i}\Vert D_{N_2,m}\Vert \rightarrow 0.$ (2) For the second term, by the C-S inequality and Assumption \ref{assumption2 .1, we have \begin{equation}\Vert R_{i,m}\Vert = \frac{\Vert E[G_{1,i,m}|F_i]\Vert }{\Vert E[G_{2,i,m}|F_i]\Vert } \le \frac{E[\Vert G_{1,i,m}\Vert |F_i]}{\Vert E[G_{2,i,m}|F_i]\Vert } \le \frac{\sqrt{C_1}}{\epsilon},\quad w.p.1.\end{equation}.}}}}}}}

elements.sent-2411-993: Note that for every $i<j$, \begin{equation}E[\langle W_{i,m},W_{j,m}\rangle] = E[E[\langle W_{i,m},W_{j,m}\rangle|F_j]]=E[\langle W_{i,m},E[W_{j,m}|F_j]\rangle]=0,\end{equation} so we can derive that \begin{equation} \begin{aligned} \mathbb{E}[\Vert \sum\limits_{i=N_2}^{k}\alpha_iW_{i,m}\Vert ^2] = \sum\limits_{i=N_2}^k{\alpha_i}^2{\mathbb{E}[\Vert W_{i,m}\Vert ^2}] \le \sum\limits_{i=N_2}^k\alpha_i^2\mathbb{E}[\Vert \mathbb{E}[G_{2,i,m}|\mathcal{F}_i]-G_{2,i,m}\Vert ^2\cdot\Vert D_{i,m}\Vert ^2] = \sum\limits_{i=N_2}^k \alpha_i^2 \mathbb{E}[\mathbb{E}[\Vert \mathbb{E}[G_{2,i,m}|\mathcal{F}_i]-G_{2,i,m}\Vert ^2\Vert D_{i,m}\Vert ^2|\mathcal{F}_i]] = \sum\limits_{i=N_2}^k \alpha_i^2 \mathbb{E}[\Vert D_{i,m}\Vert ^2(\mathbb{E}[\Vert \mathbb{E}[G_{2,i,m}|\mathcal{F}_i]\Vert ^2-2\left \langle \mathbb{E}[G_{2,i,m}|\mathcal{F}_i],G_{2,i,m} \right \rangle+\Vert G_{2,i,m}\Vert ^2|\mathcal{F}_i])] = \sum\limits_{i=N_2}^k \alpha_i^2 \mathbb{E}[\Vert D_{i,m}\Vert ^2( \mathbb{E}[\Vert G_{2,i}\Vert ^2|\mathcal{F}_i] - \Vert \mathbb{E}[G_{2,i,m}|\mathcal{F}_i]\Vert ^2 )] \le \sum\limits_{i=N_2}^k \alpha_i^2 \mathbb{E}[\Vert D_{i,m}\Vert ^2\mathbb{E}[\Vert G_{2,i,m}\Vert ^2|\mathcal{F}_i]] = C_2\sum\limits_{i=N_2}^k \alpha_i^2\mathbb{E}[\Vert D_{i,m}\Vert ^2] < \infty, \end{aligned}\end{equation} where $\langle \cdot,\cdot \rangle$ represents the inner product of two matrices, the last inequality holds because of Assumptions \ref{assumption2 .3, \ref{assumption2 .5(a) and Lemma \ref{lemma1 .}}}
elements.formula-2411-156: \begin{equation} E[\Vert \sum\limits_{i=N_2}^{k}\alpha_iW_{i,m}\Vert ^2] = \sum\limits_{i=N_2}^k{\alpha_i}^2{E[\Vert W_{i,m}\Vert ^2}] \le \sum\limits_{i=N_2}^k\alpha_i^2E[\Vert E[G_{2,i,m}|F_i]-G_{2,i,m}\Vert ^2\cdot\Vert D_{i,m}\Vert ^2] = \sum\limits_{i=N_2}^k \alpha_i^2 E[E[\Vert E[G_{2,i,m}|F_i]-G_{2,i,m}\Vert ^2\Vert D_{i,m}\Vert ^2|F_i]] = \sum\limits_{i=N_2}^k \alpha_i^2 E[\Vert D_{i,m}\Vert ^2(E[\Vert E[G_{2,i,m}|F_i]\Vert ^2-2\left \langle E[G_{2,i,m}|F_i],G_{2,i,m} \right \rangle+\Vert G_{2,i,m}\Vert ^2|F_i])] = \sum\limits_{i=N_2}^k \alpha_i^2 E[\Vert D_{i,m}\Vert ^2( E[\Vert G_{2,i}\Vert ^2|F_i] - \Vert E[G_{2,i,m}|F_i]\Vert ^2 )] \le \sum\limits_{i=N_2}^k \alpha_i^2 E[\Vert D_{i,m}\Vert ^2E[\Vert G_{2,i,m}\Vert ^2|F_i]] = C_2\sum\limits_{i=N_2}^k \alpha_i^2E[\Vert D_{i,m}\Vert ^2] < \infty, \end{equation}

elements.sent-2411-998: Define the piecewise constant interpolation function for $D_k$: $D_m^0(t) = D_{k,m , \forall t_k\le t <t_{k+1}$, $D_m^0(t) = D_{0,m , \forall t<0.$ Define the translation process $D_m^n(t) = D_m^0(t_n+t)$, $t\in(-\infty,\infty).$ Similarly define the piecewise constant interpolation function $\lambda^0(t)$ and the translation function $\lambda^n(t)$ of $\lambda$ as $\lambda^0(t) = \lambda_k,\forall t_k\le t <t_{k+1}$; $\lambda^0(t) = 0, \forall t\le t_0.$ $\lambda^n(t) = \lambda^0(t_n+t),t\in(-\infty,\infty).$ Rewrite the $m$th block of iterative equation (\ref{equation4 ) as \begin{equation}\label{eq10} D_{k+1,m}=D_{k,m}+\alpha_k(H(u_m,D_{k,m},\lambda_k)+b_{1,k}+b_{2,k}+V_{k,m}+W_{k,m}),\end{equation} where $H(u,D,\lambda) := \nabla_{\theta p(y|\theta)|_{\theta=\theta(u;\lambda) -p(y|\theta(u;\lambda))D,$ $b_{1,k := \mathbb{E [G_{1,k,m |\mathcal{F _k]-\nabla_{\theta p(y|\theta)|_{\theta=\theta_{k,m = 0,\quad b_{2,k := p(y|\theta_{k,m )D_{k,m - \mathbb{E [G_{2,k |\mathcal{F _k]D_{k,m = 0,$ $V_{k,m := G_{1,k,m -\mathbb{E [G_{1,k,m |\mathcal{F _k],\quad W_{k,m := \mathbb{E [G_{2,k,m |\mathcal{F _k]D_{k,m - G_{2,k,m D_{k,m ,$ where $b_{1,k}$ and $b_{2,k}$ are equal to 0 due to Assumption \ref{assumption2 .4.}}}}}}}}}}}}}}}}}}}}}}}}}}}}}}}}
elements.sent-2411-999: Two other terms of Equation (\ref{eq10 ) are similarly defined; for simplicity, we omit the definition part of the negative numbers: $ V_m^n(t) = \sum\limits_{i=n ^{m(t_n+t)-1}\alpha_iV_{i,m ,\ W_m^n(t) = \sum\limits_{i=n ^{m(t_n+t)-1}\alpha_iW_{i,m .}}}}}
elements.sent-2411-1001: If there exists an integer $d$ such that $t=t_{n+d -t_n$, then \begin{equation} \begin{aligned} \rho_m^n(t) = H_m^n(t)-\int_0^{t}H(u_m,D_m^n(s),\lambda^n(s))ds=\sum\limits_{i=n}^{m(t_n+t)-1}\alpha_iH_{i,m}-\int_0^{t_{n+d}-t_n}H(u_m,D_m^n(s),\lambda^n(s))ds = \sum\limits_{i=n}^{m(t_{n+d})-1}\alpha_iH_{i,m}-\int_{t_n}^{t_{n+d}}H(u_m,D_m^n(s-t_n),\lambda^n(s-t_n))ds = \sum\limits_{i=n}^{n+d- 1}\alpha_iH_{i,m}-\int_{t_n}^{t_{n+d}}H(u_m,D_m^0(s),\theta^0(s))ds = 0. \end{aligned}\end{equation} The last equality sign comes from the definition of $H_{i,m}$: $H_{i,m = H(u_m,D_{i,m ,\lambda_i).$ If there exists an integer $d$ satisfying $t_{n+d <t_n+t<t_{n+d+1}$, then \begin{equation}\begin{aligned} \rho_m^n(t) = \sum\limits_{i=n}^{n+d-1}\alpha_iH_{i,m}-\int_{t_n}^{t_{n}+t}H(u_m,D_m^0(s),\theta^0(s))ds=-\int_{t_{n+d}}^{t_{n}+t}H(u_m,D_m^0(s),\theta^0(s))ds. \end{aligned}\end{equation} Also by Assumptions \ref{assumption2 .1, \ref{assumption2 .3 and \ref{assumption2 .4, $\Vert \nabla_{\theta p(y|\theta)|_{\theta = \theta(u;\lambda_k)}\Vert = \Vert \mathbb{E [G_{1,k,m |\mathcal{F _k]\Vert \le \sqrt{C_1}$, $\Vert p(y|\theta(u;\lambda_k))\Vert =\Vert \mathbb{E [G_{2,k,m |\mathcal{F _k]\Vert \le \sqrt{C_2 .$ Furthermore, note that \begin{equation}\begin{aligned} \Vert H(u_m,D_{k,m},\lambda_k)\Vert = \Vert \nabla_{\theta}p(y|\theta)|_{\theta=\theta(u;\lambda_k)}-p(y|\theta)D_{k,m}\Vert \le \sqrt{C_1}+\sqrt{C_2}\Vert D_{k,m}\Vert \le \bar{C},\ w.p.1, \end{aligned}\end{equation} where the last equality sign comes from Lemma \ref{lemma2 with $\Vert D_{k,m}\Vert$ being uniformly bounded.}}}}}}}}}}}}}}}}
elements.sent-2411-1002: This leads to $\Vert \rho_m^n(t)\Vert \le\Vert \int_{t_{n+d ^{t_n+t H(u_m,D_m^0(s),\theta^0(s))ds\Vert \le\alpha_{n+d}\bar{C .$ This holds for almost every orbit, the right end being independent of $t$ and $u$.}}}}

elements.sent-2411-1004: Let $M_{n,m =\sum_{i=0 ^{n-1}\alpha_iV_{i,m}$, so \begin{equation} \begin{aligned} \mathbb{E}[M_{n,m}|\mathcal{F}_{n-1}]=\sum_{i=0}^{n-1}\alpha_i\mathbb{E}[(G_{1,i,m}-\mathbb{E}[G_{1,i,m}|\mathcal{F}_i])|\mathcal{F}_{n-1}] = \sum_{i=0}^{n-2}\alpha_i(G_{1,i,m}-\mathbb{E}[G_{1,i,m}|\mathcal{F}_i]) = M_{n-1,m}. \end{aligned}\end{equation} Thus $M_{n,m}$ is a martingale for every $m$.}}
elements.sent-2411-1005: Note that for every $i<j$, $\mathbb{E [\langle V_{i,m ,V_{j,m}\rangle] = \mathbb{E [\mathbb{E [\langle V_{i,m ,V_{j,m}\rangle|\mathcal{F _j]]=\mathbb{E [\langle V_{i,m ,\mathbb{E [V_{j,m |\mathcal{F _j]\rangle]=0,$ so we can derive that: \begin{equation} \begin{aligned} \mathbb{E}[\Vert M_{n,m}\Vert ^2] = \mathbb{E}[\Vert \sum\limits_{i=0}^{n-1}\alpha_iV_{i,m}\Vert ^2] = \sum\limits_{i=0}^{n-1}\alpha_i^2\mathbb{E}[\Vert V_{i,m}\Vert ^2] = \sum\limits_{i=0}^{n-1}\alpha_i^2\mathbb{E}[\Vert G_{1,i,m}-\mathbb{E}[G_{1,i,m}|\mathcal{F}_i]\Vert ^2] = \sum\limits_{i=0}^{n-1}\alpha_i^2\mathbb{E}[\mathbb{E}[\Vert G_{1,i,m}-\mathbb{E}[G_{1,i,m}|\mathcal{F}_i]\Vert ^2|\mathcal{F}_i]]=\sum\limits_{i=0}^{n-1}\alpha_i^2\mathbb{E}[\mathbb{E}[\Vert G_{1,i,m}\Vert ^2-2\langle G_{1,i,m},\mathbb{E}[G_{1,i,m}|\mathcal{F}_i]\rangle+\Vert \mathbb{E}[G_{1,i,m}|\mathcal{F}_i]\Vert ^2|\mathcal{F}_i]] = \sum\limits_{i=0}^{n-1}\alpha_i^2(\mathbb{E}[\mathbb{E}[\Vert G_{1,i,m}\Vert ^2|\mathcal{F}_i]]-\Vert \mathbb{E}[G_{1,i,m}|\mathcal{F}_i]\Vert ^2) \le\sum\limits_{i=0}^{n-1}\alpha_i^2\mathbb{E}[\mathbb{E}[\Vert G_{1,i,m}\Vert ^2|\mathcal{F}_i]] \le\sum\limits_{i=0}^{n-1}C_1 \alpha_i^2 < \infty . \end{aligned}\end{equation} The right-hand side is independent of $m$.}}}}}}}}}}}
elements.formula-2411-158: \begin{equation} E[\Vert M_{n,m}\Vert ^2] = E[\Vert \sum\limits_{i=0}^{n-1}\alpha_iV_{i,m}\Vert ^2] = \sum\limits_{i=0}^{n-1}\alpha_i^2E[\Vert V_{i,m}\Vert ^2] = \sum\limits_{i=0}^{n-1}\alpha_i^2E[\Vert G_{1,i,m}-E[G_{1,i,m}|F_i]\Vert ^2] = \sum\limits_{i=0}^{n-1}\alpha_i^2E[E[\Vert G_{1,i,m}-E[G_{1,i,m}|F_i]\Vert ^2|F_i]]=\sum\limits_{i=0}^{n-1}\alpha_i^2E[E[\Vert G_{1,i,m}\Vert ^2-2\langle G_{1,i,m},E[G_{1,i,m}|F_i]\rangle+\Vert E[G_{1,i,m}|F_i]\Vert ^2|F_i]] = \sum\limits_{i=0}^{n-1}\alpha_i^2(E[E[\Vert G_{1,i,m}\Vert ^2|F_i]]-\Vert E[G_{1,i,m}|F_i]\Vert ^2) \le\sum\limits_{i=0}^{n-1}\alpha_i^2E[E[\Vert G_{1,i,m}\Vert ^2|F_i]] \le\sum\limits_{i=0}^{n-1}C_1 \alpha_i^2 < \infty . \end{equation}

elements.algorithm-2411-006: \caption{(NMTS for training likelihood and posterior neural networks)} \label{algor:3} \begin{algorithmic}[1] \State Input: data $Y$:$\{Y_t\}_{t=1}^T$, prior $p(\theta)$, iteration rounds $K$, number of outer layer samples and inter layer samples: $M$, $N$. \For {$k \text{ in } 0: K-1$} \State Simulate $\theta_{m}$ from $q_{\lambda_k}(\theta)$ for $m=1:M$; \State Sample $\{X_{m,i}\}$ and calculate the corresponding output $y_{m,i} = g(X_{m,i};\theta_{m})$ for $i=1:N$ and $m=1:M$; \State Train $p_{\phi_k}(y|\theta)$ with a faster speed: $\phi_{k+1} = \arg\min_{\phi} -\frac{1}{MN}\sum_{m,i} \log p_{\phi}(y_{m,i}|\theta_{m}).$ \State Train $q_{\lambda_k}(\theta)$ with a slower speed: $\lambda_{k+1} = \arg\max_{\lambda}\mathbb{E}_{q_{\lambda}(\theta)}[\log p_{\phi_k}(Y|\theta) + \log p(\theta) - \log q_{\lambda}(\theta)].$ \EndFor \State Output: posterior $q_{\lambda_{K}}(\theta)$. \end{algorithmic}
